videos.title: Life in SARAJEVO! - Land of EXTREMELY BEAUTIFUL Women and PRESTINE NATURE - BOSNIA DOCUMENTARY VLOG
videos.description:
Bosnia and Herzegovina is a Balkan country near the Adriatic Sea, known for its rich history and cultural mix of Muslims, Christians, and Jews. The capital, Sarajevo, is famous for its Ottoman-style bazaar, old trams, and the site of the assassination that started World War I. The country has beautiful rivers, forests, and a small coastline. Sarajevo is safe, but public transport is poor, and many businesses only accept cash. Prices can be high, but wages are low. People are friendly, modern, and proud of their culture. Bullet holes on buildings remain from past wars as a tribute to history. While tourism is growing and the city is lively, problems like weak infrastructure, low salaries, and poor healthcare make it hard for long-term living. Still, for short visits, Bosnia offers peace, natural beauty, and a unique blend of Eastern and Western cultures.

videos.transcript: In the Balkans near the Adriatic Sea lies a country called Bosnia and Herzuggovena. Despite its rich history, it remains relatively unknown to some people. Some of you might be familiar with the country or may have even visited it. It is a former Yugoslav Republic with one of the shortest coastlines in the world where Muslims and Christians coexist peacefully. Notably, this is the location of the assassination that sparked World War I. Additionally, it was the first country in Europe to use electric trams. Geographically, Bosnia and Herzuggoa covers an area of 51,000 km. When comparing its size to that of its neighboring Balkan countries, we see that it is smaller than Serbia, but nearly the same size as Croatia. In contrast, it has a significantly larger area than Montenegro. As for its population, Bosnia and Herzuggoa currently has about 3.5 million residents. The most well-known cities include the capital SVO as well as Mustar and Tusla. Sievo located in the central part of the country is the most populous city with a population of approximately 700,000 people including the surrounding villages. If we take a closer look at the map of the capital, we can see that the central structure of the city resembles a rectangle. When you visit Sievo, you will notice the crowds of people and the traffic flow between the eastern and western parts of the city. The old historical bazaars which are some of the most crowded and popular tourist spots are located in the eastern part while the business centers are situated in the west. The Miljaka River also known as the Sievo River flows through the center of the capital and divides the city into two. Like many Balkan and European cities, Sievo is built around a river. Today, approximately 45% of the population identifies as Muslim, primarily Bosnjaks from the central region of the country. The southern part is predominantly inhabited by people of Croatian origin, making up about 17% of the population who are mostly Croatian Catholics. In the northern and eastern regions, Serbs represent the majority and about 38% of the population is Orthodox Christian. In other words, Bosnia and Herzgoa is a place where people of various religions live together happily. Some even refer to its capital as the Jerusalem of Europe, particularly in relation to Sarvo, where the Jewish community has a strong presence. For hundreds of years, Christians, Muslims, and Jews have lived together harmoniously in this city. The most significant event in the recent history of Bosnia and Herzuggovena was the assassination of France Ferdinand, Duke of Austria, Hungary, by the Serbian nationalist Gabrielo Princip. Shortly after this assassination, a series of diplomatic and military actions unfolded among the great powers, leading to the inevitable outbreak of the First World War. This historic site has now become a museum for tourists, and it attracts large crowds, making it one of the first stops for visitors to the country. Following the break up of Yugoslavia, Bosnia and Herzuggovena experienced intense armed conflicts with both Serbs and Croats across various regions. The death toll from these wars varies by source, but it is estimated that at least 200,000 people were killed and around 2 million were displaced in the struggle for independence. The currency used in Bosnia and Herzuggovena is the Bosnian mark abbreviated as KM. If you plan to visit this country, be aware of a common issue that travelers face. More than half of the businesses do not accept credit cards. Regardless of whether you are in a tourist area or a more remote part of town, this applies to small shops as well as larger restaurants, some of which even display signs indicating they only accept cash. While restaurants do accept euros, the exchange rate is such that one euro is equivalent to two Bosnian marks. You can typically use credit cards only for hotel bills and supermarket purchases. If you want to understand why some businesses do not accept credit cards, one valid reason is that they may be trying to avoid taxes. Since state tax authorities cannot easily audit these sales, businesses in Bosnia and Herzuggoa may choose not to use credit cards to avoid bank commissions and lower tax liabilities. If you set aside the issue of credit cards and visit the bazaar, you will quickly encounter one of the most recognizable symbols of Sievo, the tourist area known as Basharia. Perhaps the most iconic symbol of the city is its fountain. Visitors often take pictures in front of this fountain, much like the people you may see there. However, if you visit Sarievo during the spring or summer months, it may be challenging to get a clear photo without people inadvertently appearing in the frame. Upon closer inspection of the fountain, you will notice its distinct Ottoman architectural style. After conquering Bosnia and Herzuggovena, the Ottoman Empire constructed mosques, inns, towers, and fountains throughout the country. Despite the significant war that Sarievo endured in the 1990s, the fountain in the main bazaar survived intact and has thus become an iconic symbol of the city. In addition to the fountain, you will also find several mosques and other fountains in the bazaar where people can access free drinking water. As you walk around the city, it's often difficult to distinguish between tourists and locals. The tourist demographic in Bosnia and Herzuggoa mainly consists of people from nearby Balkan countries who share similar genetic backgrounds. The most frequent visitors are Turks, Serbs, and Croats, and you'll encounter a wide range of ages among the people in the city. Let's focus on the people in the capital, particularly in the city center. Young people on the streets tend to be positive and have an open outlook on life. They enjoy interacting with foreigners. You'll notice many well-groomed and modern young women. Some may wear headscarves while others have their hair uncovered. Bosnian women generally have light or fair skin, and a few might wear colorful burkas as part of their religious beliefs. The majority of people in Bosnia and Herzgoina have blue eyes and black hair. Most women stand between 160 and 170 cm tall and are not overweight. As for the men, they also tend to have fair skin, predominantly brown eyes, and are generally tall. Unlike some cultures, they do not traditionally grow long beards and are often clean shaven. Overall, both men and women in Sievo appear to be modern and well-groomed. As you can imagine, Bosnian, Serbian, and Croatian are the most widely spoken languages in Bosnia and Herzuggoa. The pronunciation and vocabulary of these three languages are often very similar. Additionally, you'll commonly hear Turkish expressions in Sievo, a city that reflects a 450year history of Ottoman rule. As a result, many locals understand and speak Turkish well. In fact, some businesses even hang Turkish flags in their windows to attract tourists to their restaurants. Throughout Sarvo, you will also see flags of Bosnia and Herzuggovena in many places as people here are quite nationalistic and display a strong pride for their flag. The name Bosnia literally means water. If you were to use the word water to describe a country, Bosnia and Herzuggovena would be the perfect choice, especially when traveling between cities. You will find waterfalls and rivers flowing almost everywhere. And this abundance of water leads to the country being heavily forested and wooded. The name Herzuggovena comes from a medieval duke. On the other hand, you won't find large squares in Sievo. In the capital of many countries, a big square is a common sight. However, in Sievo, you will mostly encounter long streets, covered bizaars, and cafes nestled in narrow spaces. The architecture you see around the city tends to reflect a more European style. When you visit Sievo, you'll likely come across a fire burning along the way. This is known as the monument of the unquenchable fire or the eternal flame which serves as a memorial to the fallen of World War II. The flame has been burning since 1946 and has only been extinguished once and that was by drunken youths at night. However, once bystanders noticed what was happening, they quickly relighted the fire. This monument is situated at the intersection of Tito Street and is a popular spot for visitors. Now, let's discuss living conditions in Sievo. One of the essential components of a capital city is its transportation system. I would be misleading if I claimed that transportation in Bosnia and Herzuggovena is highly developed. Unfortunately, both Sievo and the country as a whole lack a robust transport system. For instance, despite being the capital, Saraho does not have an adequate metro network, which is a significant limitation given its large geographic area. They wanted to address their metro needs with rail train systems. It's important to note that rail train systems are not the same as subways. These tramlike systems operate above ground and are not particularly smooth because they share the road with cars, which contributes to traffic congestion. In contrast, developed cities typically have subway systems that run underground and are independent of vehicular traffic. Most cars on the road are mid-range models, and some are more than 15 years old. While there is an intercity train system, its operating hours are limited. For this reason, I recommend renting a car to avoid the inconvenience of the buses, which are at least 20 years old and very uncomfortable. Another frustrating aspect is that ticket sellers at the bus terminal may not accept credit cards and will definitely insist on cash payments. The minimum monthly salary in the country is approximately €619 Bosnian marks around €300 which is an extremely low amount. Sieo in particular is not cheap for a Balkan city especially in tourist areas prices can be quite high. This is particularly true for souvenir sellers who may quote you prices of 40 or 50 for nearly any item you ask about. The average monthly income in Bosnia and Herzuggoina is between 1,00 and 1,500 Bosnian marks, which means that professionals like nurses, teachers, or civil servants typically earn around €700 per month. Monthly rents in the city often range from 1,000 to 2,000 Bosnian marks, suggesting that someone looking to rent a home will need to pay at least €500 per month. Therefore, to earn and save money in Bosnia and Herzuggoina, you would need to make at least €2,000 per month. One aspect I appreciate about this country is that men and women have equal opportunities in the workforce. If you're considering a move to Bosnia and Herzuggovena and work in construction, it could be a beneficial choice. As the country is open to development, there is ongoing construction, building and repair activity, particularly in the capital, indicating a demand for construction and installation workers. On a different note, many buildings in the country from the Yugoslav era are marked by bullet holes. If you're unfamiliar with the country's history, you will mistakenly think these holes are mere decorations. In reality, they are stark reminders of past conflicts. After the war, the Bosnians chose not to restore the facades of many buildings that had been heavily damaged. This decision was driven by the desire to honor their martyrs and keep their memories alive. Bosnia and Herzuggovena, particularly Sievo, is considered one of the safest places in the Balkans. The likelihood of experiencing something bad in Sievo is quite low. You can walk the streets at any time of day without fear. The faces of the people reflect a sense of prosperity, although the economy is not particularly strong, and there are few well-known brands aside from telecom. However, if you seek peace, quiet, and a green environment, this country can offer you just that. Bosnia and Herzuggoina features a rich mix of cultures where Muslims, Jews, and Christians live together happily. Religion is not a barrier to living in Bosnia and Herzuggovena. Everyone is free to practice their beliefs without interference. You are free to dress as you wish and can move about in both alcoholic and non-alcoholic spaces. In fact, you will encounter very few police officers or checkpoints while exploring Sievo as daytime crimes are exceedingly rare. I should mention that in the capital, if you visit places where most locals go, you generally won't encounter refugees or illegal immigrants, as the country doesn't have that issue. Occasionally, you might come across people from the Roma community asking for money. From what I've heard, the political situation in the country is quite chaotic. However, I encourage you to focus on enjoying the beauty of the country. If you earn more than €2,000 a month, you can comfortably live in Saro for an extended period and purchase an inexpensive car for only a few thousand. It's important to note that Bosnia and Herzuggovena is currently one of the five poorest countries in Europe alongside North Macedonia, Ukraine, Kosovo, and Muldova. Additionally, the health and education systems in the country are not in the best condition. In conclusion, while Bosnia and Herzuggovena, particularly Sievo, is great for a short vacation, it may not be suitable for raising a family long-term due to the various issues with essential services and the difficulty in finding employment. This brings us to the end of the video. See you in the next one. Goodbye.

videos.transcript: What's up guys! We're here in Sarajevo, and in this video, we're going to show you our top things to do here. So hope you enjoy it. Sarajevo is located in the heart of Bosnia and Herzegovina. It's the fascinating capital city of the country, where East meets West. This is because throughout its history, Sarajevo has been a crossroads of civilizations and empires, resulting in a blend of Ottoman and Austro-Hungarian architecture as well as traditions. In this video, we highlight 10 of the best things to do in and around Sarajevo. Now let's dive in! We are Zac and Ine. We make helpful videos about destinations around the world so you can easily plan your own adventures. One of the best ways to experience Sarajevo is by sampling its unique cuisine, characterized by a mix of Mediterranean and Central European dishes heavily influenced by Turkish cuisine, the food here is a must-try. Classics include ćevapi or grilled minced meat; burek which is a pastry filled with meat; and traditional soup also called Čorba. We explored this flavorful cuisine on a food tour, during which we were also introduced to Bosnian coffee, baklava made with pistachios, kadaif, and a refreshing corn-based drink. You can find a link to the same tour that we did in the description below the video. Located on top of a hill overlooking Sarajevo, the Yellow Fortress offers some of the best panoramic views of the city. This historic fortification is very popular at sunset with visitors sitting on its ramparts. Thus, it's best to arrive early to secure a prime spot. The Fortress is a short but steep walk from the Old Town, making it easily accessible. At the time of our visit, it was also free of charge. On the outskirts of Sarajevo, Bosna Spring offers a peaceful escape into nature. This expansive park is nestled along the Bosna River and boths lush greenery, crystal-clear waters, and picturesque walking trails with wooden bridges. It's a popular gathering place for locals and tourists alike. At the time of our visit, the park charged a small entrance fee. If you're coming from the city center, you can either drive your own car, take a taxi, or take the tram number 3 to the neighboring town of Ilidža. You can easily walk or take a horse ride along the treelined promenade to the main entrance. Soaring high above Sarajevo, Mount Trebević offers some of the most elevated views of the city and surrounding landscapes. To reach the summit, you'll need to take a cable car, and the station can be found a short walk away from Sarajevo's Old Town. The ride to the top is very scenic with nature and city views the whole way up. Once at the summit, you can explore hiking trails or simply relax and soak in the beautiful scenery. Our favorite view was from the Sarajevo Viewpoint, which is just to the east of the cable car station. From the top of the cable car, it's a 10-minute walk to the Bobsled Tracks, so you can easily combine both activities.µ Located on the slopes of Mount Trebević, you can find the abandoned bobsleigh and luge tracks from the 1984 Winter Olympics. This iconic landmark is now covered in graffiti and has been partially reclaimed by nature. You can walk on the tracks and explore its twists and turns, while imagining the thrill of Olympic athletes who once competed here. If you're looking for some more action, head to the nearby Sunnyland, as they have a 600-meters long alpine coaster that you can actually take a ride on. Diving into Sarajevo's recent history is a moving but essential experience. There are a number of museums that are dedicated to war and conflict, each with a unique focus. We visited Gallery 11/07/95, a memorial dedicated to the victims of the Srebrenica genocide, one of the darkest chapters in recent European history. More specifically, in July 1995, over 8,000 Bosniaks were killed in and around the town of Srebrenica, which had been declared a UN Safe Area at the time. In the museum, you can find a collection of photographs, personal belongings, and documentary evidence which showcases these horrors. Other museums in town also provide further insights into this complex history, such as the Crimes Against Humanity and Genocide 1992 to 1995 exhibition, the Siege of Sarajevo Museum, and the War Childhood Museum. Sarajevo has a religious diversity that shapes the city's character and everyday life. It's a place where the call to prayer blends with church bells, and a walk through the Old Town quickly reveals this contrast. Visit the Gazi Husrev-beg mosque, a masterpiece of Ottoman architecture, which stands out as a symbol of Islamic heritage. You can also explore the Orthodox Church nearby, with its beautifully decorated interior. And walking through the neo-Gothic Sacred Heart Cathedral is also a must. On the outside of this Cathedral, you can also see remnants of shelling that happened during the war. Finally, the old Synagogue, which is now the Museum of the Jews of Bosnia and Herzegovina tells the story of the country's Jewish Community. Sarajevo's Old Town offers an interesting mix of history, architecture, and cultural delights. Wander its cobblestone streets and take in some of the most important sites of the city. These include the iconic Latin Bridge, the grand Emperor's Mosque, the elegant Sarajevo City Hall, the Eternal Flame, and the Ottoman-style wooden fountain. You can also find the exact location where Franz Ferdinand was assassinated, which is one of the sparks that ignited the First World War. If you truly want to dive into the history of the city and these sites, we recommend going on a guided walking tour. Find a link to the 3-hour tour that we joined in the description below the video. Sarajevo can get pretty hot in the summer, but luckily there's a lot of historic water fountains scattered throughout the city. So definitely recommend bringing a reusable water bottle when you visit. Baščaršija is the heart of Sarajevo's Old Town and walking around here feels like traveling back in time. This historic bazaar is a labyrinth of cobblestone streets, lined with Ottoman-era architecture, Mosques, and traditional craft shops. It's a sensory overload with the aroma of freshly-brewed Bosnian coffee mingling with sights of colorful textiles. Don't miss the Food Street and the coppersmith shops lining one of the oldest streets in the city. Located beneath a seemingly ordinary house near Sarajevo Airport, the Tunnel Museum is a reminder of the city's resilience during the Bosnian War. During the Siege of Sarajevo, an 800-meter long tunnel served as a lifeline, providing a route for food, supplies, and people to enter and exit from. Today, the house with the tunnel has been transformed into a museum. Exhibits showcase a collection of wartime artifacts, photographs, and documents that portray the challenges faced by the city's residents. You can also watch a documentary film about the war and take a look at the original tunnel. Right next to it, there's also a replica tunnel that you can actually pass through. While Sarajevo is undeniably fascinating, a day trip beyond the city allows you to take in the full beauty and diversity of Bosnia and Herzegovina. If your time is limited, consider venturing out to experience the country's stunning landscapes and charming towns. For hiking and fresh mountain air, head to Lukomir. But for for picturesque towns, choose the popular Mostar and Konjic route, or the historic Jajce and Travnik route. You can find some tour options from Sarajevo to these places in the description below the video. And if you're planning your Bosnia and Herzegovina trip, we recommend keeping things organized with our interactive travel planner. We also included other important links in the description that will help you with your Sarajevo experience, such as an interactive map so you can get to each place that we met mentioned. We'll catch you on the next adventure!

2. Gazi Husrev-beg Mosque – the largest historical mosque in Bosnia and Herzegovina
3. Sarajevo Clock Tower – A unique clock and a must-see landmark near the mosque.
4. Sarajevo City Hall – a blend of psuedo-Moorish Revival architecture, now a library and museum.
5. Jajce Barracks – Once a royal residence, it is now an abandoned former military barracks
6. White Fortress – For panoramic views of Sarajevo, the White Fortress is an ideal spot, offering a blend of history and natural beauty.
7. Sacred Heart Cathedral – A striking Catholic cathedral located in the heart of the city.
8. Free Walking Tours – Discover Sarajevo’s hidden gems, history, and culture with a local guide
9. Yellow Fortress – Enjoy stunning sunset views over Sarajevo from this fortress, a peaceful yet poignant site with a great view.
10. Assassination Place of Franz Ferdinand – Learn about the event that sparked World War I at the exact spot where Archduke Franz Ferdinand was assassinated.
11. Siege of Sarajevo Museum – Dive into the history of Sarajevo’s resilience during the longest siege in modern warfare.
12. Museum of Crimes Against Humanity and Genocide – An emotional but important museum that sheds light on the atrocities of war.
13. War Museum 1992 Sarajevo – Learn about Sarajevo’s recent history and the impact of the Bosnian War at this poignant museum.
14. Eternal Flame Monument – A tribute to the city's liberation from Nazi occupation and symbol of Sarajevo’s enduring spirit and a tribute to those who lost their lives.
15. Abandoned Bobsleigh Track – A relic of the 1984 Winter Olympics, this track, now covered in graffiti, is a unique and eerie destination for urban explorers.

videos.transcript: Sarajevo, a city where East and West don’t just meet—they embrace in harmony; I never expected such warmth, history, and soul woven into every street. In this guide, I’ll show you everything you need to know before visiting Sarajevo— where to go, what to eat, how to get around, and hidden gems that will blow your mind. By the end of this video, you’ll have the most important tips at your fingertips, and I’ll be waiting for your likes and any specific questions in the comments. Sarajevo is best known for its deep historical layers—from Ottoman mosques to Austro-Hungarian facades and scars left by the 1990s war. Nestled in a mountain valley in the heart of Bosnia and Herzegovina, it feels like a peaceful cradle of cultures. The city offers visitors a powerful blend of soulful architecture, traditional food, moving museums, and spiritual encounters like the call to prayer echoing through Bascarsija. Start your Sarajevo journey at Vekil Harčova Mosque, a peaceful and historic corner often missed by tourists. Cross the graceful Šeher-ćehaja Bridge, one of the oldest Ottoman bridges spanning the Miljacka River. Nearby stands the majestic Sarajevo City Hall, a true symbol of resilience with its grand Austro-Hungarian design. Walk through Kazandžiluk, the coppersmith street where artisans still hammer tradition into souvenirs. Pause at Baščaršija Mosque, a tranquil oasis amidst the hustle of the old bazaar. Just steps away, the Brusa Bezistan surprises with textiles and stories from centuries past. Don’t miss the iconic Sebilj Fountain, always buzzing with pigeons and people. The Old Serbian Orthodox Church offers a glimpse into the city’s religious mosaic. Stop by Morića Han, once an inn for traders, now a charming place for tea. The grand Gazi Husrev-beg Mosque stands at the heart of Sarajevo’s spiritual life. Look up at the nearby Clock Tower, which still tells time according to lunar hours. Wander through Gazi Husrev-beg's Bezistan, a cool stone market perfect for browsing local goods. The Museum of the Jews of Bosnia and Herzegovina reveals a rich, often overlooked heritage. Stroll along Ferhadija Street, the vibrant pedestrian artery of the city. Visit Ferhadija Mosque, one of the oldest Islamic buildings in Sarajevo. For a sobering experience, the Museum of Crimes Against Humanity and Genocide is an essential stop. Marvel at the Sacred Heart Cathedral, a masterpiece of Neo-Gothic architecture. Across the street, Gallery 11/07/95 deeply moves with its haunting tribute to Srebrenica. Rest at Liberation Park, where locals relax among monuments and trees. The Serbian Orthodox Church of the Nativity of the Theotokos stuns with its pastel-colored facade and golden domes. Dive into history at the Gazi Husrev-Beg's Museum, tucked beside the mosque. Next door, the Gazi Husrev-beg Library is a treasure trove of ancient manuscripts. Cross the historic Latin Bridge, where a world-changing moment unfolded. The Sarajevo Museum beside it gives insight into the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand. Admire the Academy of Fine Arts, housed in a former Evangelical church with a scenic bridge in front. Step into the serene Emperor’s Mosque, the oldest in the city. Finally, hike up to Bijela Tabija, the White Fortress, for panoramic views over Sarajevo that are truly unforgettable. Sarajevo International Airport is the main gateway, with connections from cities like Istanbul, Vienna, and Belgrade. If you're traveling from neighboring countries like Croatia, Serbia, or Montenegro, international buses or scenic drives through the mountains are common options. Trains are limited but improving, and the drive into the city, especially from Mostar or Dubrovnik, is nothing short of spectacular. Within the city, Sarajevo is very walkable—especially the old town, which is best explored on foot. Trams and buses are affordable and connect most parts of the city, while apps like MojTaxi or renting a scooter can be handy. I recommend renting a car if you're planning to visit surrounding gems like Vrelo Bosne, Mostar, or the Olympic mountains. The local currency is the Bosnian Convertible Mark (BAM), and most places accept card, but cash is still handy. Bosnian is the main language, though many young locals speak English, and EU or US citizens can enter visa-free for up to 90 days. Tipping around 10% at restaurants and cafés is appreciated, and remember to dress modestly if you’re visiting mosques or religious sites. In Sarajevo, you can wander through centuries of history, enjoy panoramic hikes, and sip coffee in atmospheric courtyards where time seems to slow down. The most unique part of the experience is how the city blends Ottoman, Austro-Hungarian, and Yugoslav influences in everyday life—from architecture to daily rituals. One of the must-do things is simply sitting with locals, watching life pass by, and soaking in the rhythm of a city that feels both soulful and raw. Sarajevans are warm, curious, and incredibly welcoming to travelers, often eager to help or share stories with a smile. The food culture is rich, hearty, and deeply rooted in traditions, where long meals and generous hospitality are a norm. Shopping in Sarajevo is centered around small artisan bazaars and family-run shops, where bargaining isn’t aggressive but rather a slow, respectful dance. Spring (April–June) and autumn (September–October) are ideal for pleasant weather, fewer crowds, and colorful scenery. Summers are vibrant and lively, perfect for festivals, but can get hot and a bit crowded. Winters turn Sarajevo into a snowy fairytale, especially for those interested in skiing or cozy cultural experiences with fewer tourists around. Compared to major European cities, Sarajevo is very affordable—expect to pay a third or less of what you'd spend in Paris or Vienna. A typical tourist might spend around €40–€60 per day including food, entrance fees, transport, and mid-range accommodation. Meals at restaurants range from €6–€10, local transport is under €1, and coffee rarely exceeds €1.50. Yes, Sarajevo is perfect for budget travelers—with hostels starting around €10–€15 per night, cheap eats under €5, and plenty of free or low-cost attractions. Local bakeries, markets, and walking tours offer great value without compromising on experience. On a tight budget, you can enjoy Sarajevo for as little as €25–€30 per day, covering food, lodging, and transportation. In summer, lightweight clothes and breathable fabrics work best, while in winter you’ll need warm layers, waterproof shoes, and a good coat for the snow. Bring comfortable walking shoes year-round, as many streets in the old town are cobbled and hilly. While there’s no strict dress code, modest attire is appreciated in religious sites like mosques—so it’s good to pack a scarf or cover-up just in case. The main area to explore is the historic center around Baščaršija, where culture, cuisine, and architecture all come together in one walkable zone. From Ottoman courtyards to Austro-Hungarian streets and socialist-era blocks, this area reflects Sarajevo’s layered history. Most tourist attractions, cafés, and museums are concentrated here, making it the perfect base for first-time visitors. Sarajevo offers a rich mix of grilled meats, stews, pastries, and fresh breads, with meals that feel home-cooked and full of flavor. Street food is everywhere, but I highly recommend trying ćevapi, I had mine at the famous Ćevabdžinica Željo, and it lived up to the hype. While traditional dishes dominate, there are also vegan-friendly spots, modern cafés, and cozy fine-dining restaurants scattered across the city. Handcrafted copperware, traditional coffee sets, and locally made wool socks make great souvenirs or gifts. You’ll find authentic shopping experiences inside the old bezistans, historic covered bazaars filled with artisans and small vendors. Bargaining is acceptable in a friendly way, and don’t miss picking up some locally produced honey, soaps, or sweets to take home. Most travelers stay in or near Baščaršija for easy access to attractions, food, and culture, with options ranging from luxury hotels to boutique guesthouses. Airbnb rentals are also popular and often located in charming old buildings or modern apartments with a view. I stayed at Hotel Herc, which was just a short walk from the heart of the city and offered both comfort and great value. Nightlife in Sarajevo is relaxed but full of soul, with everything from cozy taverns and local pubs to trendy bars and live music spots. You’ll find locals and tourists mixing in the old town, especially around Ferhadija and Baščaršija, where the vibe is friendly and authentic. Don’t miss the chance to enjoy a late-night coffee or rakija while live sevdah music plays in the background. For a short stay, two full days are enough to explore the old town, visit key museums, and get a taste of local culture. If you have more time, spending four to five days lets you discover surrounding areas like the Olympic mountains, spring of the Bosna River, and nearby towns like Mostar. Sarajevo rewards those who linger—it’s not just a city to see but to feel. Sarajevo is generally safe, even for solo travelers, and locals are helpful and respectful toward tourists. Common scams are rare, but as in any city, it’s wise to watch your belongings in crowded areas and avoid unregistered taxis. In case of emergency, dial 112 for immediate help, which connects to all essential services. Absolutely—Sarajevo is one of Europe’s most underrated gems, offering rich history, emotional depth, and unforgettable cultural experiences. It’s a place where every corner tells a story, and the people make you feel at home. This guide has everything you need to know before visiting Sarajevo, so don’t forget to like, comment, and share this video—and if you have questions or need help planning your trip, feel free to ask in the comments below and subscribe for more inspiring travel guides!

A city that's creative, complicated, and quietly extraordinary. It's not slick or predictable, but that's exactly why it stayed with us. >> This is the Europe you don't expect, and it might just change the way you see the rest of it. >> I'm Judy. >> And I'm Kevin. Welcome back to Finding Gene Marie, where we share our lives as full-time travelers and the connections we make along the way. Sievo is the capital of Bosnia and Herszoggoina, surrounded by mountains on all sides and divided by the Milakja River. >> I always like when there's a river in a city. It gives us a point of reference that someone can say just walk towards the river and you know to turn left or right to figure out where you're going. >> We stayed near the Academy of Fine Arts just across the river from Oldtown. It was quieter and a little cheaper, but Oldtown has the pulse of the city. The bridge near the academy is part art, part shortcut. Locals call it Fina Lente. Make haste slowly. >> The city even had to add stoppers to keep skateboards from looping through the metal ark. >> While neighborhoods climb high into the hills, most of what you'll want to see. Oldtown museums, cafes, and markets sit right at the base of the valley. This area gets a four out of five on our mobility scale. >> Just a few minutes walk from the river, locals gather in Liberation Square. We were delighted to discover a chess board the size of a living room and locals gathered for a serious game of giant chess. There's plenty of good-natured ribbing and kibbitzing from the sidelines. For a city that's lived through so much, it's comforting to see that the only kind of battle happening here now is one built on laughter, strategy, and friendship. In most European cities, the old town belongs to tourists. So, it was a surprise and honestly a joy to see how many locals are still woven as Sievo's old town. >> This is Bachia. The name means the main market or bizaar and it's been the city's heart since the Ottomans ruled here in the 1400s. After dinner with friends one night, we introduced them to Bosnian coffee. The cafes were full of locals, not tourists, and we loved it. >> Just steps away is a Cilia Fountain where horses once drank and people still do. And no matter when you visit, there are always pigeons. >> Down Copper Smith Alley, you hear the same rhythm you would have heard four centuries ago. The steady tap of hammers. It's a trade that's been passed down in families for generations. >> These lines I just made here. You know, >> we were struck by the resilience and ingenuity of these craftsmen who transformed bullets and bombshells from the War of the 1990s into vessels of beauty and art. Our stomachs were growling, so we decided to make our way to Bereanicha Sach, where they bake the city's favorite comfort food, burek, which is made the old-fashioned way, coiled in a spiral and cooked under a satch, a heavy bell-shaped lid covered in coals. >> Are we good? >> I think so. Now, it it is hard because I kind of do want to taste the spinach and feta, but the meat is always so good. So, this will be I feel like a a true truer comparison with most. >> Yeah. And it's one of our guidelines that we don't follow all the time is I'm a bit hangry. So, searching around for restaurants, we should have done this a little bit before. >> It just took us longer than we wanted it to to find this particular spot. >> It happens. >> Ask for a side of yogurt for dipping. >> All right. So, I guess if you get the whole ring, that's a kilogram. >> Yeah. Which which I think would be quite a lot. Yeah, >> it's tasty. Um I feel like it was this is a little soggy compared to what we had in um Mostar. >> Oh, but it's good. I like it. And you said this was cooked under the dome, which is on all the signs. >> Under the bell. >> Under the bell. Sorry. >> So you can see it in the signs and the lights. kind of looks like a walk with a cover. >> It was flaky, rich, and is the kind of simple food that tastes like someone's grandmother still makes it. It's traditional, hearty, and comforting. >> Expect to hear the call to prayer from the Gaza Beg Mosque. And a few minutes later, church bells from the Neo Gothic Sacred Heart Cathedral where Pope John Paul II came to pray after the war. >> The cathedral was reconstructed after the war. The damaged parts were reconstructed. But this part, they left it like this on purpose for commemoration. >> Not far from there are the copper domes of the Serbian Orthodox Church. There's also a Jewish synagogue standing quietly nearby. In most cities, this mix might feel unusual, but these religions coexist peacefully side by side. We give Oldtown a four out of five for mobility. Here on Ferhadia Street is a simple brass line that marks where east meets west literally. On one side are the domes, minouetses and low Ottoman buildings of Basaria. On the other is Austrohungarian facades pastel elegant. These buildings would be right at home in Vienna. What's extraordinary isn't that these worlds exist side by side. It's that they still do. Sieo never chose one identity over the other. It learned to hold both. Just across the river from city hall stands one of Syria's most charming landmarks. A small wooden house called Inaca, the house of spite. When the Austrohungarians planned this area in the 1890s, one homeowner refused to sell. After weeks of pleading, he finally agreed on one condition. Every stone and beam had to be moved piece by piece across the river. The rebuilt house still stands today, but it's now a Bosnian restaurant. This quiet act of defiance captures Sievo itself. Pushed, tested, yet always standing its ground. We met up with Mark and Joanne, fellow full-time travelers at Nanina Kuina, a restaurant known for its authentic Bosnian comfort food. You'll want to make sure to get a reservation in advance. I went for the goulash Judy had Dolma, grape leaves stuffed with rice and meat, and we shared a shopska salad. We were so glad for the opportunity to slow down and share meaningful conversation with friends. If you follow the river, you'll come to a corner near the Latin Bridge, which is where Archduke France Ferdinand and his wife Sophie were assassinated in 1914, setting off World War I. A generation later, the eternal flame was lit to honor Siegeo's resistance against fascism in World War II. It's never gone out, not even during the siege of the 1990s, and it's come to symbolize the city's enduring spirit. After the world wars, Sieo became part of socialist Yugoslavia under President Tito. Not Soviet style communism, but its own path between east and west. You can still see that era in the city's concrete housing blocks and modernist buildings. They're not particularly dazzling, but they were built to be sturdy, longlasting, and affordable. When Siebo hosted the 1984 Winter Olympics, those same ideals of progress and unity took center stage. Today, those buildings look plain and utilitarian. But to the people of Sievo, the reminders of a time when the city looked forward. We took a tour that brought us face to face with the part of Sievo most travelers only read about. Our guide, Salom, was a veteran who fought through the siege, a war he said they were never supposed to win. He lost his brother, his cousin, and many friends. There was no official army, just neighbors and fathers defending their homes. We weren't soldiers, he told us. The fight was only supposed to last 7 days, but it lasted 4 years. Our first stop was an ordinaryl looking house, hiding something extraordinary beneath it. The entrance to Sievo's tunnel of hope, the lifeline that kept the city alive during the siege. The walls outside are still pockmarked with bullet holes, reminders of how close the front lines once came. Across the road, hidden behind a wall of vines, solemn brush back the leaves and showed us what's left of another entrance, a collapsed tunnel opening on the city side. When the city fell under siege in 1992, Sieva was completely cut off. It had no power, no supplies, no safe way in or out. The United Nations controlled the airport and brought in humanitarian aid, but civilians couldn't get to it. So Sieans did something extraordinary. They dug a tunnel beneath the airport by hand in secret and under sniper fire, working around the clock for months. It connected the city to the free Bosnian territory on the other side. Through this narrow passage came everything the city needed to survive. Food, medicine, fuel, weapons, even hope. The tunnel ceiling was so low the soldiers had to crouch as they moved through kneedeep mud and icy water carrying 50 lb loads in darkness. >> This is not easy. I can't imagine doing this with equipment. >> Imagine >> and the cold and the water, the mud. >> I don't even know. And they did that for years. Amazing people. Soomn said Sievo didn't survive because it was stronger. It survived because it refused to give up. >> So, wake up call, huh? >> Yeah. I think it's just incredibly powerful the motive that the Bosnian people had to fight for their country and um >> save their family. >> Save their families. >> They had the motivation. The other side, they said the only motivation was to kill. >> Yeah. It it's terrible and hard to fathom and just like even building this tunnel as an opportunity uh to uh go and get supplies and everything. Where would this country be if they didn't have that? They they would not have lasted. >> No. And they wouldn't have had the border still open on the mountain side, which is what saved them from getting completely blocked in. >> Yeah. Very powerful. From there, we drove along what used to be Sniper Alley, the main road that cuts through Sievo. The city sits in a narrow valley surrounded by four mountains, breathtaking, but deadly once the hills filled with artillery. From the hills above, snipers could see nearly every street. Afterwards, we drove to the yellow fortress above Oldtown. We could see why Sieva was so vulnerable. It's a city in a bowl, surrounded on all sides by mountains. Those same slopes once echoed the cheers from the 1984 Olympics less than a decade before the siege. Now looking out over the city, it's hard not to feel both awe and disbelief. >> Back on the ground, Sievo remembers in quieter ways. >> Look down at the sidewalks and you'll eventually notice small bursts of red in the pavement. The roses of Sieo. >> Each one marks where lives were lost during the siege of the 1990s. They're quiet memorials the city has learned to live alongside. They're outside schools and cafes, near street corners and parks. Soomle even pointed out one outside the entrance to the Tunnel of Hope Museum. Sieo doesn't try to hide its wounds. It builds around them. If you ever thought Sievo was frozen in time, just look at its walls. We took a street art and history tour, and Allan showed us how even the concrete buildings that once were gray and uninviting have become canvases. After the war, the city didn't cover its scars. It gave them color. Murals of musicians, tributes to victims, surreal scenes, and everyday life like this vintage car stretch across cracked facades. Reminders that life goes on. >> The street art here isn't rebellion. It's resilience. Not every part of Sievo could afford to be restored. So, local artists get the permission of tenants of damaged buildings that are in the back streets of the city to transform the damage into art. They host festivals to raise funds for the paint and scaffolding, turning tragedy into beauty. And then for 20 years and eventually he decide to bury Danto. So at that time I feel it that I need to paint something and this was a lot. I came almost alone. It was just one guy helped me. Wow. To paint it and I decide to be on this place to have this like the shape of the background to look a bit destroyed and stuff like this. It's my view of it. [Music] We decided to take the cable cars up to the summit of Tribic Mountain. So, you're going to take the cable cars and it's going to take you to the top of the mountain, but you have to work for it because there's plenty of stairs first. Let's go. >> It's a very warm day today. Almost as warm as yesterday. So, we'll see how cool it is at the top. Certainly hot on the stairs. Oh, but look, little benches on the You get tired on the way up. >> 35 steps. >> There you go. >> I lost count, so I don't know for sure, but it was at least 103 steps. Could have been more. >> There was a road you can go via cab or whatever to get to the back of the cable car station. So, that is a way to avoid the steps. In 1984, Sievo hosted the Winter Olympics, the first ever held in a socialist country. It was a moment in time for pride and unity. A decade later, those same slopes became front lines during the siege as Yugoslavia was being ripped apart. The bobsled run turned into a bunker, and the road up the mountain was lined with landmines and sniper fire. [Music] And here's where we watch Judy go down the slide. We >> It's uh doesn't look like a wee. It looks like ow ow ow ow ow. Bob sledding or Bob walking. >> Oh gosh. I thought it was Kevin. >> You have no bobbing. >> Oh gosh, that's terrible. >> I wouldn't want to slip down this in the winter, though. >> I think you would go all the way down. The bobsled track, despite being cracked and overgrown, now is an open air gallery covered in murals. What once was a front line, is now a place for picnics and art. It's proof that joy can return to even the unlikeliest places. One of our viewers told us a story about a rare 600-year-old Jewish manuscript, the Serieo Hagda. It's a richly illustrated Jewish text used during Passover that's housed in a small room inside the National Museum. It's only on display on Tuesdays and Thursdays from noon to 100 p.m. For centuries, people of every faith risked their lives to protect this rare book. Muslims, Catholics, Jews through wars and occupations. In more recent times, a Muslim librarian risked his life to hide it from the Nazis who came for it by saying that it had already been turned over. He ended up protecting it by hiding it in what some say were the floorboards of a mosque. This single act of humanity captures Sievo's essence. Ordinary people protecting things because they are important. When you want a quick but traditional meal in between sightseeing, one of our favorite everyday meals was chapi with onions, warm bread, and a side of fries. The sausages here were the perfect texture, and the location was great. Sievo isn't trying to be the next big thing. It may not be as popular as Vienna or Istanbul, though you'll see traits of both. And it's not a place where you build a checklist of top 10 things to see and do. But we're really glad we visited and learned from people like Alan and Soomn and shared time with fellow travelers. >> Sieo's history and its people are profound and inspiring, and we came away different and better from having walked its streets. This city just keeps being itself. It's layered, surprising, and unhurried. Sievo refuses to choose just one identity and we think it's stronger for it. >> Before we go, if you're curious about what it actually cost to spend 24 days in Sievo, here's what we spent. This is proof that meaningful travel doesn't have to be expensive. It cost $36 to take a bus from Mostar to Sievo. Our accommodations near the university across the bridge from Oldtown were $1,147, which breaks down to $48 per night. We originally planned to spend a month, so the book cost us $38 per month, but we had to pivot to the US to see a sick family member. Taxi rides from the bus station to the airport cost $59. Restaurants and groceries were a total of $65, which breaks down to be $25 per day. Tours and activities were $94. That's a grand total of $1,941. Excluding travel from MostAR, the total is $1,95, which breaks down to $79 per day or $40 per person per day. This makes Sievo one of the most cost-effective places in Europe we've ever stayed. If you're curious what resilience looks like beyond Sievo, check out our video from Mostar, one of the most beautiful and interesting cities in the Balkans. >> Thanks for watching. If you haven't already subscribed, we'd love for you to do that. >> And remember to >> wander more, >> worry less, >> and bring a passport. >> Until next time. Until next time.
